narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path despite challenges, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admission system uses a model trained on data from thousands of students like you, looking at factors such as early and later school grades, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPA, family income, and whether you'd study full-time to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you specifically, the model estimated a 47.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for denial since the school prioritizes applicants predicted to pass with high confidence. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, very close to the typical student's 3.21, was the single strongest factor pulling the prediction toward success, showing your solid college performance stood out positively. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1—much lower than the average of around 5.5—had the biggest negative impact, strongly pushing the model toward predicting failure by signaling a dip in later academic performance compared to peers. Your lower-middle family income bracket, below the dataset average, offered some favorable influence, as the model associates it with resilience in similar students who succeeded. Your LSAT score of 23, well below the typical 36.7, added a moderate push toward the failure prediction, highlighting it as an area where you differed from stronger applicants. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also lower than average, contributed a smaller negative nudge, while your choice of full-time study aligned with the vast majority of students (over 92%) and slightly favored passing. Overall, while your GPA and background provided some uplift, the lower deciles and LSAT were the key drivers tipping the scale against you compared to typical admits who rank higher in school performance and test scores. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many students improve with targeted effort. Next, I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, seeking tutoring or strategies to elevate your school rankings if possible, and highlighting any personal achievements or hardships in a revised application to future schools. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing forward with that strength we see in you.
